text: The surgical team dissects along the inferior border of the pancreas using a left-hand bipolar grasper. A small venous tear occurs, and attempts are made to control the bleeding using bipolar energy and a vessel sealer, though initially both methods are ineffective.

The surgeon exchanges the vessel sealer for a needle driver and loads a 4-0 proline suture with a lapar tie into the abdomen. A simple figure of eight suture is demonstrated to control the bleeding from the arterial or venous injury.

During the robotic Whipple procedure, the surgeon encounters bleeding at the gastroduodenal artery (GDA) stump on the specimen side. The dissection continues to develop the portal vein tunnel, and initial attempts to control the bleeding with bipolar electrocautery are shown to exacerbate the bleeding.

The surgeon uses a grasper in the left hand to apply pressure to the area of arterial bleeding. The rescue stitch is deployed, and a figure of eight suture is placed to control the bleeding. Both arms are used in tandem to maintain gentle traction on the suture during the second throw, minimizing bleeding.

The surgeon performs an additional third throw of the figure of eight suture to control bleeding, maintaining tension on the suture to prevent further hemorrhage. Difficulty in grasping the needle is addressed by using the pancreatic parenchyma on the specimen side for stabilization. After achieving bleeding control, a laparotide is placed to secure the suture, and the procedure is converted to open due to anticipated challenges in further dissection along the portal vein.
